name: Craftsman Knowledge Quiz
description: Validates that the craftsman agent integrates knowledge from all specialist disciplines

questions.question: You are about to implement a new feature. What is the very first thing you do?
questions.expectedAnswer: Write a failing test that describes the desired behavior
questions.rubric: Must mention writing a failing test FIRST, before any production code

questions.question: You have a class that handles both user validation and email sending. What principle is violated and how do you fix it?
questions.expectedAnswer: SRP is violated. Extract email sending into its own class/module. Each class should have one reason to change.
questions.rubric: Must identify SRP violation and recommend extraction into separate responsibilities

questions.question: You wrote a function named 'process'. What is wrong and how do you fix it?
questions.expectedAnswer: The name is not intention-revealing. Rename to describe what it actually does, e.g., 'calculateMonthlyInvoiceTotal' or 'validateUserRegistration'.
questions.rubric: Must identify the naming problem and provide a specific, intention-revealing alternative

questions.question: A specialist reviewer says your code has a DIP violation: your high-level business logic directly instantiates a database connection. How do you fix this?
questions.expectedAnswer: Introduce an abstraction (interface/protocol) for the data access. The business logic depends on the abstraction. The database implementation is injected.
questions.rubric: Must describe introducing an abstraction and using dependency injection

questions.question: Two specialists give contradictory advice. The SOLID specialist says to extract an interface for a class with only one implementation. The Architecture specialist says the abstraction is unnecessary at this scale. What do you do?
questions.expectedAnswer: Evaluate which principle serves the code better in this specific context. If there is no foreseeable variation point, the Architecture specialist's advice likely wins -- YAGNI. Document the trade-off.
questions.rubric: Must show ability to weigh competing advice contextually rather than rigidly following one

questions.question: Your test passes but you notice the production code could be cleaner. Your next step?
questions.expectedAnswer: Refactor. You are in the green phase of Red-Green-Refactor. Clean the code while all tests pass.
questions.rubric: Must identify the refactor step of the TDD cycle

questions.question: You need to add error handling to a function. Should you use error codes or exceptions? Why?
questions.expectedAnswer: Exceptions. Error codes force the caller to check return values (easy to forget), clutter the happy path, and violate the command-query separation. Exceptions separate error handling from normal flow.
questions.rubric: Must recommend exceptions and explain why they are preferred over error codes

questions.question: You are working on a feature and notice a poorly named variable in nearby code you did not write. What do you do?
questions.expectedAnswer: Rename it. The Boy Scout Rule: always leave the code cleaner than you found it.
questions.rubric: Must reference the Boy Scout Rule or the principle of continuous improvement
